# Smart textiles as an opportunity for the textile industry

Digitalization

Digitalization

# Smart textiles as an opportunity for the textile industry

Digitalization

20.07.2020

A time-honored industry that has slipped into crisis in recent decades could gain renewed momentum thanks to new technologies: Smart sportswear, innovative medical products or intelligent jackets will soon be highly sought-after products. This will require know-how from many different areas to be brought together.

It may be hard to believe today - but the textile industry was once the largest industrial sector in Austria with the most employees. The companies were always highly innovative and developed most splendidly in Vorarlberg and in Upper Austria, but also in the Waldviertel or in Styria. Globalization has changed this fundamentally in recent decades: Whereas in 1955 88 percent of textiles sold in this country were "made in Austria", in 2004 this figure was only 18 percent. The trend is still downward.

In 1960, the Austrian textile and clothing industry still had around 100,000 employees; in 2018, it had fewer than 15,000. The companies that remain have focused on niches - for example, on technical textiles, as suppliers for automotive manufacturers, in the direction of functional sportswear or on highly specialized fibers - in which they are also quite successful. The gross value added of the sector has stabilized at around 700 million euros in recent years.

## A T-shirt that feels the pulse

Now a new future field is appearing on the horizon: so-called "smart textiles". These are textiles that are made "intelligent" by integrating information technology: They react to changes and adapt to them. These textiles contain sensors that sense environmental changes, they exchange data with cell phone apps or the cloud, for example, and in some cases they have actuators that cause a desired change in the textile.

An example might be a T-shirt that registers pulse, respiration rate, heart rate, and geodata without requiring any additional device, such as a smartwatch or chest strap; this could be very interesting for sports or occupational safety, for example. Another possible application is bedding that detects body fluids (such as blood or urine) and, if necessary, sends an automatic alarm to caregivers; in elderly care or nursing, this could be very helpful.

Many areas of application are conceivable, such as automatically cooling mattresses. Or innovative building technology such as measuring moisture on flat roofs, heating or illuminating wall textiles, and measuring snow loads on roofs. Furthermore, gas sensors can be integrated into work clothing to warn of hazards. Leisurewear can be fitted with control elements, e.g. for smartphones. And jackets for schoolchildren or emergency services could light up automatically at night. An item of clothing thus becomes a "wearable" in the truest sense of the word.

## Fibers that generate electricity

Ideally, all the necessary functionalities are integrated directly into the fibers or fabrics. This starts with piezoelectric sensor threads that convert mechanical energy (such as stretching) into electricity, and extends to soft, flexible batteries or energy storage in yarns that act like capacitors, through to fabrics on which electronic circuits or antennas are printed or embroidered. The particular challenge here is that the smart textiles must also be able to keep up with conventional fabrics in terms of flexibility, wearing comfort, washability and durability - and that they do not cause any problems when recycled or disposed of at the end of their service life.

The global market volume of "smart textiles" is estimated to be in the order of 40 billion dollars by 2030. In terms of Austria, this could mean an annual gross value added of 475 million euros, according to a study just published by researchers at the AIT Austrian Institute of Technology. So the question arises as to whether the domestic textile and apparel industry could also leverage this potential? In several workshops, the status quo, opportunities, expectations and possible barriers were systematically examined together with industry representatives and experts. As a result of the project, prerequisites and measures were listed so that Austria could take on a pioneering role in the field of "Smart Textiles".

The study was commissioned by the Federal Ministry for Climate Protection, Environment, Energy, Mobility, Innovation and Technology (BMK) and funded by the Austrian Research Promotion Agency (FFG); support was provided by the "Smart Textiles" platform.

Basically, the experts see the greatest potential in the areas of protective and emergency clothing, medical and care textiles (including bandages and orthotics), and sports and outdoor clothing. The analysis of several scenarios showed that many existing companies could benefit from "smart textiles" if they invest in research and development at an early stage, if they network with relevant players to supplement their own missing competencies, and if they can differentiate themselves from competitors as a result. In less optimistic scenarios, traditional companies only manage to tap the new technological potential for themselves to a limited extent; instead, new companies could enter the market to serve the smart textiles market.

![Word Cloud on the topic of smart-textiles](/fileadmin/_processed_/4/c/csm_Smart_Textiles_-_Wordcloud__710568510f.jpg "Smart Textiles Studie")

## Cooperation between textile and electrical industry

A key issue here is research and development: On the one hand, research spending and research capacity in the textile industry are comparatively low. "But companies need to invest now, at a time when these technologies are still in their infancy," says project manager Beatrix Wepner from the AIT Center for Innovation Systems & Policy. On the other hand, know-how needs to be built up in a completely different area. So far, it has not been sufficiently successful to bring together the competencies for the development of smart textiles, is a central conclusion of the study. "Many of the identified topics can only be addressed in close cooperation between the textile industry and the electrical industry," says Wepner. From the textile industry's point of view, there is currently a lack of suppliers and development partners, especially from the electronics and software sectors, he said.

The establishment of a cross-industry "Smart Textile Innovation Ecosystem" is therefore necessary. Suggestions include targeted research funding programs and the development of "lighthouse applications" that publicize the possibilities of the new technologies, for example in the care or medical sectors. Networking and persuasion are considered essential in order to win over not only textile companies but also players from other relevant sectors for the topic. The participatory development of a "Smart Textiles Roadmap" with the involvement of customers and users is also considered to be useful. In addition, training courses in the field of "electronic textiles" could be set up at technical universities, technical colleges and vocational schools - with the aim of creating a separate occupational profile of "textronics engineer".

Ultimately, all these measures should lead to a stimulation of entrepreneurial investments and activities in the field of smart textiles. "Due to the Corona crisis, the textile industry has regained importance locally. In Vorarlberg, for example, ad hoc certified reusable medical masks were developed to minimize dependence on other countries," Wepner said. "This shows the great potential that exists in Austria. Now it's a matter of linking and promoting this."

--- SOURCE 3: Smart Regional Investments in Textile Innovation ---
URL: https://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/policy/communities-and-networks/s3-community-of-practice/partnership_industrial_mod_smart_regional_investments_textile_en

SUMMARY:
The Partnership aims to strengthen the regional innovation capacities, to facilitate investment in open innovation infrastructures or new technologies.

FULL CONTENT:
![European Commission](https://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/assets/images/logo/logo-ec--mute.svg)

# Smart Regional Investments in Textile Innovation

![alternative text](https://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/images/policy/communities-and-networks/s3-community-of-practice/smart_regional_textile.jpg)

### Context

Today, the textile and clothing industry counts 143,000 companies, mostly SMEs, employing 1.3 million people (5% of EU manufacturing employment) generating a turnover of EUR 147 billion (3% of EU manufacturing value added) widely spread across the EU. However, a substantial portion of (mostly lower added value) textiles and clothing consumed in the EU is produced elsewhere in the world, often in Asia, taking advantage of very low labour costs. Disadvantages of this model include long-distance vulnerable supply chains, poor working conditions in "sweat-shops", and high environmental costs.

Looking at the share of the partner and observer regions in the general employment in the textile and clothing sector, they constitute a total of 28% of employment in the textile industry and 23% in clothing, which makes the partnership an important player in the modernisation of the sector in Europe.

**Year established:** 2016

### Regions (lead and partner)

![](https://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/images/policy/communities-and-networks/s3-community-of-practice/MAP_TEXTILE.jpg)

### Mission

The Partnership's mission is to bring together stakeholders from the textile, clothing and related industries (companies, research, education, public bodies and agencies) in a joint effort to develop and implement strategies and projects meant to facilitate and accelerate the emerging industrial renewal in traditional manufacturing regions across Europe.

By 2025, textile industry, including fibre-based materials, clothing, home and technical textiles, should be a strategic EU industry sector providing innovative and competitive products enabling personalised and attractive solutions.

### Objectives

The Partnership is co-developed by member EU regions with an active participation in textile industry, involving the full quadruple helix of players, so to better connect regional authorities with industrial interests and needs.

The Partnership aims to strengthen the regional innovation capacities, to facilitate investment in open innovation infrastructures or new technologies, and to establish effective collaboration between EU actors, so to offset the risks and costs associated with long-distance supply chains.

### Main activities

The Partnership employs a value chain approach along related priority domains of smart specialisation in member regions with the aim to create an investment pipeline of mature projects relying on new cross-sectoral industrial value chains developed by multiple stakeholders, so to achieve lasting improvements, in areas like:

![](https://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/images/policy/communities-and-networks/s3-community-of-practice/textile-figure.png)

### Organisations involved

Belgium

Czechia

France

Italy

Portugal

Romania

Spain

Sweden

the Netherlands

Other

--- SOURCE 4: Our Mission - Smart Textile Alliance ---
URL: https://smarttextilealliance.com/our-mission/

SUMMARY:
Our mission is to catalyse and accelerate the growth of smart textiles by cultivating cross-industry collaboration and supporting development of voluntary,

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/STA-logo-top_black.png)

# Our Mission

Our mission is to catalyse and accelerate the growth of smart textiles by cultivating cross-industry collaboration and supporting development of voluntary, consensual standards. This will ensure interoperability of electronics and textile components within the structured stack and mature the smart textile supply chain.

## Sustainable e-textiles

Recent global focus on tackling the impacts of textile production, textile and electronic waste led to instigation of a wide variety of initiatives to facilitate behavioural changes in consumption and circular innovations to promote reuse and recycling.

Integration of electronic circuitry, sensors and batteries for innovative functionality in e-textiles increase environmental and manufacturing costs that are best mitigated via a modular approach.

By working for development of interoperable plug and play components and use of sustainable materials, STA fosters a modular approach that can facilitate replacement of defect components and disposal/recycling of electronic components via modification of existing e-waste schemes.  
.

![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/growth2.jpg "growth2")

## Industry Standardization

Many industries, including electronics and textiles, have faced similar challenges, but successfully overcame them through formation of independent standards bodies and certification programmes.

Standards organizations shifted the needle on these industries; giving consumers the confidence they needed to buy these products and resulted in the strong and healthy growth of these industries.

This is exactly what is needed for smart textiles companies to grow. And precisely why we founded Smart Textile Alliance. Our vision is to make electronics soft and textiles smart to accelerate the cross-industry growth.

Our aim is therefore to develop industry standards based on leading smart textile technologies to support responsible and sustainable product development, activities that are align with the roadmap for the Smart textile Stack.

![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Peripherals-1.png "Peripherals")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Wireless.png "Wireless")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Fair-Trade.png "Fair Trade")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/WiFi-1.png "WiFi")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Web.png "Web")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Care-Labels.png "Care Labels")
![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/Handling_grafik_rev.jpg "Handling_grafik_rev")

## The Smart Textile Stack

The Smart Textile Stack is a framework to group components that are important to standardize.

With this layered approach and a full implementation of supportive industry standards, developers of smart textiles should only care about sensor development, software applications and design of the smart textile product.

## Opportunities

STA works to promote consensual industry standards for smart textile components to ensure their interoperability. We do so through collaborative R&D efforts, and by taking part in funded programmes.

We have extensive experience of putting together successful proposals and projects, so if you have a project idea that might be relevant for other companies in the smart textile supply chain too, you are welcome to get in touch with us to hear what opportunities might be available.

![](https://smarttextilealliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/STA-logo-bottom.png "STA-logo-bottom")

We promote plug and play solutions that contribute to making electronics soft and textiles smart. The Smart Textile Alliance is not only for tech companies, it is also a platform for fabric and garment manufacturers to find new partnerships.

+44 20 7843 9494

--- SOURCE 5: The Future of Fashion Collaborations: Limited Editions and Cross ... ---
URL: https://ayerhsmagazine.com/2025/07/26/the-future-of-fashion-collaborations-limited-editions-and-cross-industry-partnerships/

SUMMARY:
Consider the burgeoning collaborations between fashion and technology. Smart textiles, wearable devices, and augmented reality (AR) try-on

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://ayerhsmagazine.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/ayerhs-logo-1.png?w=1024)
![](https://ayerhsmagazine.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/39.jpg?w=1024)

# The Future of Fashion Collaborations: Limited Editions and Cross-Industry Partnerships

*by [brownfashionagal](https://www.instagram.com/brownfashiongal/)*

Type your email…

Subscribe

The fashion landscape is in a constant state of flux, driven by evolving consumer demands, technological advancements, and a relentless pursuit of novelty. In this dynamic environment, fashion collaborations have emerged as a powerful strategy, transcending traditional boundaries and reshaping the industry as we know it. No longer merely a marketing gimmick, collaborations have become a cornerstone of brand innovation, cultural relevance, and commercial success. At the forefront of this evolution are two distinct yet interconnected trends: the proliferation of limited-edition drops and the burgeoning landscape of cross-industry partnerships. These strategies, when artfully executed, ignite consumer desire, generate unprecedented hype, and redefine the very essence of brand identity in the 21st century.

### The Allure of Scarcity: Limited Editions as a Driving Force

The concept of “limited edition” is far from new in the luxury market, where exclusivity has always been a hallmark of prestige. However, in recent years, this strategy has been democratized and amplified, becoming a dominant force across all segments of the fashion industry, from high-end couture to accessible streetwear. The psychology behind this phenomenon is deeply rooted in human desire for what is rare and unattainable. By intentionally restricting supply, brands cultivate a powerful sense of urgency and desirability, often triggering what is colloquially known as FOMO – the Fear Of Missing Out.

This scarcity model is meticulously orchestrated, often involving highly anticipated “drops” that sell out in minutes, sometimes even seconds. The immediate sell-out creates a secondary market, further fueling the hype and perceived value of these coveted items. Consumers are no longer just buying a product; they are acquiring a piece of cultural currency, a testament to their quick reflexes and access to exclusive networks. This phenomenon has given rise to a dedicated community of collectors and resellers, transforming fashion from a purely utilitarian or aesthetic purchase into an investment and a social statement.

Limited editions offer a myriad of benefits to fashion brands. Firstly, they generate immense buzz and media attention, often without the need for traditional advertising. Social media platforms become fertile ground for viral marketing, with consumers eagerly sharing their successful acquisitions and lamenting their near misses. This organic engagement is invaluable, reaching a far wider audience than conventional campaigns. Secondly, limited drops allow brands to experiment with new designs, materials, and creative directions without the full financial commitment of a regular collection. This agile approach fosters innovation and keeps the brand fresh and relevant. Thirdly, the heightened demand often translates into premium pricing, significantly boosting revenue and profitability. Finally, successful limited editions can significantly enhance brand reputation, positioning the brand as desirable, innovative, and connected to the cultural zeitgeist.

However, the limited-edition strategy is not without its challenges. The intense pressure to secure an item can lead to consumer frustration and brand fatigue if not managed carefully. Brands must strike a delicate balance between generating hype and maintaining accessibility to avoid alienating a segment of their audience. Moreover, the environmental implications of rapid production and consumption cycles, even for limited runs, are increasingly under scrutiny. As sustainability becomes a paramount concern for consumers, brands employing limited-edition strategies must integrate eco-conscious practices, from material sourcing to production processes, to ensure their efforts align with evolving ethical standards.

### Beyond Boundaries: The Rise of Cross-Industry Partnerships

While limited editions often involve collaborations within the fashion sector itself (e.g., designer x streetwear brand), the future of fashion collaborations is increasingly defined by partnerships that transcend industry boundaries. These cross-industry alliances bring together seemingly disparate entities, resulting in unexpected and often groundbreaking creations that captivate diverse audiences. The rationale behind such partnerships is multifaceted: to tap into new demographics, to inject fresh perspectives and expertise, to amplify brand messaging, and to create truly innovative products or experiences.

Consider the burgeoning collaborations between fashion and technology. Smart textiles, wearable devices, and augmented reality (AR) try-on experiences are no longer futuristic concepts but tangible realities. Fashion brands are partnering with tech companies to integrate functionality and interactivity into their garments, blurring the lines between clothing and gadgetry. This can range from athletic wear that monitors performance to luxury items embedded with payment capabilities or enhanced digital experiences. These collaborations not only elevate the product offering but also position fashion at the forefront of technological innovation, appealing to a tech-savvy generation.

The entertainment industry is another fertile ground for cross-industry fashion collaborations. Musicians, filmmakers, and gaming companies are increasingly lending their creative vision and immense fan bases to fashion brands. Capsule collections inspired by popular movies or video games, concert merchandise designed by high-fashion labels, or virtual fashion skins for avatars in the metaverse are becoming commonplace. These partnerships leverage the emotional connection consumers have with their favorite entertainment franchises, translating that loyalty into fashion consumption. The storytelling potential is immense, allowing brands to weave narratives that resonate deeply with their target audience.

Beyond technology and entertainment, we are witnessing a rise in collaborations with sectors like food and beverage, automotive, and even home decor. These seemingly unconventional pairings offer brands the opportunity to explore new aesthetics, experiment with unique materials, and engage consumers in unexpected ways. A luxury fashion house collaborating with a renowned chocolatier to create an exquisite, limited-edition confection, or a streetwear brand partnering with an automotive manufacturer to design custom vehicle interiors and accompanying apparel, exemplifies the creative freedom and market expansion afforded by cross-industry alliances. These collaborations often blur the lines between product and experience, offering consumers something truly unique and memorable.

The success of cross-industry partnerships hinges on a few critical factors. Firstly, **authenticity and shared values** are paramount. A collaboration must feel genuine and make sense to the consumer; a forced or purely commercial pairing can backfire. Both brands must bring something unique and complementary to the table. Secondly, **creative synergy** is essential. The resulting product or experience should be a true fusion of both brands’ identities, not just a superficial branding exercise. Thirdly, **effective communication and marketing** are crucial to conveying the narrative behind the collaboration and engaging diverse audiences. Leveraging the marketing channels and fan bases of both partners is key to maximizing reach and impact.

### The Intersection of Limited Editions and Cross-Industry Partnerships

The most potent and impactful collaborations often sit at the intersection of limited editions and cross-industry partnerships. When a highly anticipated product or experience is born from the convergence of distinct industries and released in limited quantities, the resulting hype and consumer demand can be exponential. Think of a luxury car brand collaborating with a renowned fashion designer to create a custom car model and a complementary, limited-edition apparel line. The exclusivity of both the car and the clothing, combined with the prestige of both brands, creates an unparalleled sense of desirability.

These hybrid collaborations leverage the best of both worlds: the immediate gratification and cultural currency of limited drops, combined with the innovative potential and expanded reach of cross-industry alliances. They create cultural moments, pushing the boundaries of what consumers expect from brands and fostering a deeper, more emotional connection.

### Challenges and Opportunities for the Future

Despite the immense potential, the future of fashion collaborations, particularly in their limited-edition and cross-industry forms, presents both challenges and opportunities.

**Challenges:**

**Opportunities:**

### The Road Ahead: A Collaborative Ecosystem

The future of fashion collaborations points towards a more interconnected and dynamic ecosystem. Limited editions will continue to thrive, driven by the enduring appeal of scarcity and the desire for cultural relevance. However, their success will increasingly depend on a commitment to sustainable practices and a thoughtful approach to managing consumer expectations.

Cross-industry partnerships, on the other hand, will become even more diverse and imaginative. We can anticipate collaborations with unexpected sectors, blurring traditional industry lines and creating entirely new product categories and experiences. The rise of Web3 technologies, including NFTs and the metaverse, will offer new avenues for digital-only collaborations, virtual fashion, and immersive brand interactions.

Ultimately, the most successful collaborations in the future will be those that are not only commercially astute but also culturally resonant and ethically conscious. They will be driven by a genuine desire to innovate, to tell compelling stories, and to connect with consumers on a deeper level. The fashion industry, historically characterized by its fluidity and adaptability, is poised to embrace this collaborative future, continually reinventing itself through bold partnerships and the enduring allure of the limited and the unique. The age of isolated brand development is receding; the era of collaborative creativity is here to stay.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Collaborations between textiles and sectors like technology, healthcare, and automotive foster innovation, leading to smart textiles that enhance functionality and consumer appeal.
Investment in joint R&D projects among textile companies and tech firms is crucial for advancing smart textile technologies and addressing industry challenges effectively.
Emerging trends reflect consumer demands for smart textiles, prompting brands to innovate with features like wearables that monitor health and enhance user experience.
